findings: Hook (d = r * t with a concrete 400-mile drive at 50 mi/h) gives a student-visible reason to want to rearrange before any vocabulary lands.

Concept slide (04) cleanly separates the three cleanup moves multi-step equations actually require — distribute, clear fractions with the LCD, collect variables — instead of presenting one giant procedure. Each row carries a micro-example so a student can self-check on the spot.

Compare slide (05) names the two most common errors students make on this topic: distributing only the first term inside the parentheses and moving an x term without applying the same move to the other side. The right-hand column explicitly models the corrected behavior.

Worked examples (06 and 07) parallel the two assignment categories: variables on both sides + parentheses, and fractions cleared via LCD. The traces are line-by-line with a labeled inverse operation on each row, so a student can self-grade their own work.

Both pauses model the full skill: pause 1 ends with an explicit substitution check, pause 2 ends with the one-sentence plain-English explanation of what the isolated letter means. Both are exactly what the assignment rubric requires.

Final path slide ends with a concrete Learn Portal action: read OpenStax 2.4–2.5, run Khan Academy variables-on-both-sides practice, complete the dashboard's 10 multi-step + 5 literal rearrangement assignment with per-letter explanations.
watch_points: Slide 10 (literal equations) carries two side-by-side cards (A = lw and PV = nRT); keep narration pace measured so the student can read both rearrangements as they are spoken.

Slide 07 (clear fractions) uses LCD = 6 across four terms in one row; advisors should flag a student who solves it mechanically but cannot explain why multiplying both constants and fraction terms by 6 preserves the equation.
